analysis.note: The supplied bhāṣya line prints adyāḥ, but this is analyzed as ādyāḥ, “first/initial,” on grammatical and contextual grounds; literal adyāḥ from ad/adya would not make sense as the heading of this list.
A -ādi compound: “contact and the others / contact and so on.”

verification.detail: compound stem sparśādi as Pum/Prathama/Bahu: pass; relation bahuvrihi: unsupported (logged)

samasa.vigraha: chandaś ca adhimokṣaś ca smṛtiś ca

analysis.note: Coordinative compound listing three mental factors; feminine plural by final member smṛti.

verification.detail: compound stem chandādhimokṣasmṛti as Stri/Prathama/Bahu: pass; relation dvandva: unsupported (logged)

analysis.note: Indeclinable “together with,” construed with the following instrumental dual samādhidhībhyām.
Instrumental dual fixed by saha; dative/ablative dual would be formally possible but is not the construction here.

verification.detail: compound stem samādhidhī as Stri/Trtiya/Dvi: pass; relation dvandva: unsupported (logged)

analysis.note: Past-passive-participle stem from ni-√yam, used substantively as a class-heading, “the determinate/fixed ones.”
Nominative singular item beginning the next list segment in the verse.
Connective particle; its initial a coalesces with the preceding final ā in śraddhātha.
Visarga/r-sandhi before following a-: hrīḥ apatrapā → hrīr apatrapā.
Ethical mental-factor term, translated here as “dread of blame / moral compunction.”

justifications.decision: I take the opening as a nominative plural class-heading, not as a form of ad “eat” or literal adya “today.” The following nominative plural sparśādayaḥ supplies the appositional content: “the first ones are contact and the rest.”

evidence.translation: The syntax requires a nominative plural heading plus a nominative plural list-expression; “eating” or “today’s” would not construe coherently.

justifications.decision: The compound is read as bahuvrīhi-like X-ādi usage, “those whose first is contact,” hence “contact and so on,” rather than as two separate syntactic words.
options_considered: single -ādi compound meaning “contact and so on”
justifications.chosen: sparśādayaḥ as a -ādi compound: “contact and the others”

evidence.translation: The -ādi formation conventionally means “beginning with X, X and the rest.”

justifications.decision: The form lists three mental-factor nouns—chanda, adhimokṣa, smṛti—rather than making smṛti dependent on chanda/adhimokṣa as “memory of desire and resolve.” The plural nominative supports enumeration.
options_considered: tatpuruṣa: memory of desire and resolve

evidence.translation: A plural compound with final member smṛti naturally functions as a dvandva: “desire, resolve, and mindfulness/memory.”

justifications.decision: saha is treated as the indeclinable “with,” and samādhidhībhyām as an instrumental dual dvandva governed by it. The two terms are added to the preceding three under niyatāḥ.
options_considered: saha as indeclinable with instrumental dual
samādhidhībhyām as dative or ablative dual
justifications.chosen: saha samādhidhībhyām = “together with concentration and understanding”

evidence.translation: The instrumental dual with saha gives “together with samādhi and dhī.”

justifications.decision: The nominative plural niyatāḥ is read as a substantivized participial adjective referring to a class of mental factors: the five named in the surrounding phrase. It is not a finite verbal form, nor merely an adjective modifying only the feminine dvandva chandādhimokṣasmṛtayaḥ.
options_considered: masculine nominative plural substantive: “the determinate ones”
feminine nominative plural agreeing only with chandādhimokṣasmṛtayaḥ
justifications.chosen: niyatāḥ as a substantive class-heading, “the determinate ones”

evidence.translation: The phrase yields a list of five items identified as “determinate/fixed.”

justifications.decision: śraddhā, hrīḥ, and apatrapā are nominative singular feminine nouns; atha is a connective. hrīr is only the sandhi-form of hrīḥ before apatrapā.
options_considered: hrīr as an independent r-final stem
justifications.chosen: śraddhā atha hrīḥ apatrapā as three nominative list-items with a connective particle

evidence.translation: The sequence is segmented as śraddhā atha hrīḥ apatrapā: “faith, then shame, and dread of blame.”

translation:
The first are contact (sparśa) and the rest;
the determinate ones (niyatāḥ) are desire (chanda), resolve (adhimokṣa), and mindfulness (smṛti),
together with concentration (samādhi) and understanding (dhī).
Faith (śraddhā), then shame (hrī), and dread of blame (apatrapā).
one_shot_delta: A purely mechanical reading could take the printed adyāḥ as a form of ad/adya, yielding an incoherent “eating/today’s” interpretation instead of “the first.”
It could miss the enumerative force of -ādi in sparśādayaḥ: the word means “contact and the rest,” not merely “contact-first.”
It could read chandādhimokṣasmṛtayaḥ as a dependent compound, e.g. “memory of desire and resolve,” rather than a three-item dvandva list.
It could take saha from the adjectival dictionary entry “powerful,” but the instrumental dual samādhidhībhyām makes the indeclinable “with/together with” the correct construal.
It could misparse samādhidhībhyām as dative/ablative dual; the saha-construction fixes it as instrumental.
It could fail to undo the sandhi in śraddhātha and hrīr apatrapā, obscuring atha and the nominative hrīḥ.
analyzer_disagreements: ByT5 lemmatizes adyāḥ as ad; I analyze the intended form as ādyāḥ from ādya, since ad/adya gives no coherent list-heading here and the vulgate reading supports ādyāḥ.
ByT5 segments sparśa + ādayaḥ; I treat the printed unit sparśādayaḥ as a single -ādi compound stem sparśādi.
ByT5 segments chanda + adhimokṣa + smṛtayaḥ; I treat chandādhimokṣasmṛtayaḥ as one itaretara-dvandva compound.
ByT5 leaves saha essentially unanalysed; I classify it as an indeclinable because it governs/associates with the instrumental dual samādhidhībhyām.
ByT5 segments samādhi + dhībhyām; I treat samādhidhībhyām as one dvandva compound in the instrumental dual.
ByT5 gives niyatāḥ under the root niyam; I analyze the verse form as the nominal participial stem niyata, not as a finite verb.
For atha, the printed sandhi sequence is śraddhātha; I record the underlying surface as atha and the printed residue as tha.
open_questions: The prompt’s bhāṣya line prints adyāḥ while the vulgate has ādyāḥ; in this ablation I have normalized to ādyāḥ on grammatical and contextual grounds, but without commentary this remains a textual/orthographic uncertainty.
The exact technical nuance of chanda and apatrapā cannot be fixed from the supplied dictionary excerpts alone; the translation uses standard Yogācāra/Buddhist mental-factor equivalents.
